question: Describe the basic structure of a singly linked list and explain the time complexity of inserting a new node at the beginning of the list versus at the end.
expectedPoints: A singly linked list is a collection of nodes where each node contains data and a pointer to the next node.
Insertion at the beginning is an O(1) operation as it only requires updating the new node's next pointer and the head pointer.
Insertion at the end is O(n) for a list without a tail pointer because it requires traversing the entire list to find the last node.

question: Explain the primary difference between a stack and a queue. Provide one real-world application for each data structure.
expectedPoints: A stack is a Last-In-First-Out (LIFO) structure, while a queue is a First-In-First-Out (FIFO) structure.
A real-world application of a stack is the undo/redo functionality in a text editor.
A real-world application of a queue is managing print jobs in a printer spooler.

question: Compare and contrast the Adjacency List and Adjacency Matrix representations for graphs. Discuss the time and space complexity trade-offs for common operations like checking for an edge between two vertices and finding all neighbors of a vertex.
expectedPoints: An adjacency matrix uses a VxV 2D array, providing O(1) time to check for an edge but requiring O(V²) space.
An adjacency list uses an array of linked lists, requiring O(V+E) space, which is more efficient for sparse graphs.

Finding all neighbors of a vertex is O(V) in a matrix (must scan a row) but O(degree(V)) in a list (directly traversing the linked list). Checking for an edge is O(1) in a matrix but O(degree(V)) in a list.
